lessons.title: 1. Introduction to Linux & FHS
lessons.content:
Introduction to Linux & FHS

🎯 Objectives

  • Understand the File Hierarchy Standard (FHS).
  • Know the purpose of critical directories like /etc, /bin, /var.

The File Hierarchy Standard (FHS)

Linux organizes files in a single unified tree starting at the root directory /.

  • /bin: Essential command binaries (ls, cp).
  • /etc: System-wide configuration files.
  • /home: User home directories.
  • /var: Variable data like logs (/var/log).
  • /root: The root user's home directory.
  • /tmp: Temporary files (often cleared on reboot).

lessons.title: 2. Navigation & File Operations
lessons.content:
Navigation & File Operations

Basic Navigation

The shell is your primary interface. You navigate using these commands:

$ pwd       # Print working directory
$ cd /etc   # Change directory to /etc
$ cd ~      # Go to home directory
$ cd ..     # Go up one level

File Operations

  • ls -la: List all files including hidden ones.
  • mkdir folder: Create a new directory.
  • touch file.txt: Create an empty file.
  • cp source dest: Copy files.
  • mv source dest: Move or rename files.
  • rm file: Delete files (use with caution!).

lessons.title: 3. Text Processing (The Hacker's Tools)
lessons.content:
Text Processing

As a pentester, you will often need to parse large files, logs, and outputs.

Essential Tools

  • cat file.txt: Print file content to terminal.
  • grep "password" file.txt: Search for a pattern inside a file.
  • awk '{print $1}': Extract specific columns from text.
  • sed 's/old/new/g': Find and replace text.
  • cut -d ':' -f 1 /etc/passwd: Extract the first field separated by a colon.

Piping and Redirection

You can chain commands using the pipe | operator:

$ cat /var/log/auth.log | grep "Failed password" | awk '{print $11}'

lessons.title: 1. Process Management
lessons.content:
Process Management

Everything running in Linux is a process with a unique Process ID (PID).

Viewing Processes

  • ps aux: View all running processes.
  • top or htop: Interactive process viewer.

Controlling Processes

  • kill 1234: Send a SIGTERM signal to PID 1234.
  • kill -9 1234: Send a SIGKILL (force kill) to PID 1234.
  • Ctrl+C: Terminate foreground process.
  • Ctrl+Z: Suspend process (move to background).

lessons.title: 2. Users & Permissions
lessons.content:
Users & Permissions

Linux security is based on file permissions and ownership.

Permission Types

Read (r=4), Write (w=2), Execute (x=1).

Permissions apply to: Owner, Group, Others.

$ chmod 755 script.sh  # rwxr-xr-x
$ chown root:root file.txt # Change owner and group

The Root User

The root user (UID 0) has absolute power. Use sudo to execute commands as root.

lessons.title: 1. Networking Basics
lessons.content:
Networking Basics

Checking Interfaces & IP

Old way: ifconfig. Modern way: ip a.

Connections and Ports

  • ping 8.8.8.8: Test connectivity.
  • netstat -tulpn or ss -tulpn: Show listening ports and active connections.

DNS and Routing

  • cat /etc/resolv.conf: View DNS servers.
  • ip route: View routing table.

lessons.title: 2. Services and Scheduling
lessons.content:
Services & Cron

Systemd (systemctl)

Manage background services (daemons).

$ systemctl start apache2
$ systemctl enable ssh
$ systemctl status cron

Cron Jobs

Automate tasks at specific intervals.

$ crontab -e
# Format: Minute Hour Day Month DayOfWeek Command
* * * * * /path/to/script.sh

lessons.title: 1. Privilege Escalation Basics
lessons.content:
Privilege Escalation Basics

How hackers go from a low-privilege user to root.

SUID Binaries

SUID (Set Owner User ID) allows a user to run a file with the permissions of the file's owner.

Finding SUID binaries:

$ find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null

Sudo Misconfigurations

Check what you can run as sudo:

$ sudo -l

lessons.title: 2. Log Analysis
lessons.content:
Log Analysis

Logs are critical for both defense (detecting attacks) and offense (clearing tracks).

  • /var/log/auth.log: Authentication attempts (SSH logins, sudo usage).
  • /var/log/syslog: General system logs.
  • /var/log/apache2/access.log: Web server access logs.

Use tail -f /var/log/auth.log to watch logs in real-time.
